column3: He wove a catalogue of Pontiffs from public records.

You will say: In the first book of Kings, chapter 18, verse 17, and in Isaiah 22, in the time of King Hezekiah, Eliakim (who is the same as Joakim) son of Hilkiah is named as steward of the house, and is numbered among the chief priests.

I respond: This Eliakim was not the High Priest, nor indeed was his father Hilkiah; but he was one of the principal priests, or among the princes of the priests, to whom the care of the temple and its gates was entrusted, as is clear from Scripture and from Josephus, from which it is evident that there was only one Hilkiah who was Pontiff, not in the time of Hezekiah, but much later in the time of Josiah, concerning whom see 4 Kings 23:4.

Second reason, because what is said in chapter 5 concerning the number of Jews returning from captivity agrees excellently with the times of Xerxes. Likewise, that in chapter 6 the Jews are not said to have kings, but leaders of the people, Uzziah and Charmi; for if these events had occurred under Manasseh, Judith would surely have conducted this her deed with King Manasseh, not with the Pontiff Joakim, Uzziah, and Charmi, nor would Joakim have sent soldiers to occupy the mountain passes, but Manasseh.
Again, under Manasseh, Bethulia had been deserted by the Israelites carried off into Assyria, and was inhabited by the Cutheans.

Furthermore, consistent with the age of Xerxes is what is said in chapter 5, verse 27, where the nobles of Holofernes say of the children of Israel that they are now unarmed, unwarlike, and inexperienced in war, and therefore defend themselves with mountains and precipices, as is stated in chapters 7 and 8; and what is added in the final chapter concerning the long peace that followed upon this victory of Judith. So judge Sanchez here,

After Xerxes had been frequently defeated by the Greeks, and had especially received that most shameful defeat at Thermopylae at the hands of Leonidas and his three hundred companions in the fifth year of his reign, he began to be held in contempt by his own people, as Justin says in Book 3. Wherefore Arphaxad king of the Medes, who until then had been a tributary and subject to Xerxes and the Persians, seized upon this occasion to throw off the yoke of Xerxes and the Persians and to extend the borders of his kingdom. Nebuchadnezzar did likewise; to him either Xerxes himself, or more likely (as I think) Darius Hystaspis the father of Xerxes, had entrusted the kingdom of Nineveh and the Assyrians, and he therefore, making war against Arphaxad and overcoming him, began to seek sole monarchy.

Therefore this Nebuchadnezzar is not that great monarch of the Chaldeans, the author of the Babylonian captivity, but one who came much later than him, an Assyrian or Babylonian, one of the princes of Xerxes; for Nebuchadnezzar is a Babylonian or Chaldean name, and perhaps from Nebuchadnezzar the Great our Nebuchadnezzar here received this name, and perhaps also his lineage and descent.

Hence there is a conjecture as to the reason why Xerxes was so benevolent toward the Jews, as Josephus writes in Book 11, chapter 5, so much so that his son Artaxerxes in the seventh year of his reign sent Ezra, and in the twentieth year sent Nehemiah to restore Jerusalem with most liberal and ample authority; the reason, I say, of this benevolence was that Xerxes saw this Nebuchadnezzar, his enemy and rival for empire, had just recently been overthrown by the Jews.

You will say: In the time of Xerxes, Nineveh was desolate. For it had already been destroyed by Nebuchadnezzar the Great in the fifth year after Jerusalem was taken, as I have said. But it is evident from the last response of Tobias that Nineveh was rebuilt shortly after its destruction, because it was a most ancient and noble city, and this was permitted by the Babylonian or Persian kings, as history shows to have happened with Babylon, Jerusalem, Antioch, Rome, etc. Other objections I shall resolve at chapter 4, verse 5, and chapter 8, verse 1, or chapter 9, verse 2.

Finally, what is said at chapter 5:22 and following almost proves this view, that Jerusalem was desolate at this time and the temple overthrown and leveled with the ground. For this did not happen under Manasseh, but under Zedekiah in the Babylonian captivity.

From what has been said it is clear that the Nebuchadnezzar who sent Holofernes was not Darius Hystaspis, as Gerard Mercator holds in his chronology; nor Artaxerxes Ochus, as Severus Sulpicius holds; nor Cambyses son of Cyrus, who obstructed the building of the temple begun under Cyrus (1 Esdras), on the grounds that the Jews had slain his general Holofernes—so says Berosus Annianus, and this is hinted at by St. Augustine, City of God 18.26, and Eusebius; for Cambyses reigned only seven years, whereas Nebuchadnezzar sent Holofernes in the twelfth year of his reign.

Rather he was either Xerxes, as Sanchez thinks, who was called Nebuchadnezzar on account of his pride and arrogance similar to that monarch—whence the Jews in the victory-song of Judith at chapter 16:12 sang: "The Persians trembled at her constancy, and the Medes at her boldness"—or more probably he was a prefect among the princes of Xerxes who governed Nineveh in his name, as I said. For he seems to have been Babylonian by origin, as his name Nebuchadnezzar, which is Babylonian, suggests. Arphaxad in turn was a prefect or king of Media tributary to Xerxes; but when Xerxes was defeated by the Greeks, both rebelled against Xerxes and made themselves absolute kings of their respective provinces. For in just this way Artabanus rebelled against that same Xerxes, and perhaps he is Arphaxad. Hear Justin, Book 3, at the beginning: "Xerxes, king of the Persians, previously the terror of nations, after waging war in Greece with ill success, began to be held in contempt even by his own people. For Artabanus his prefect, as the king's majesty declined daily, was led to hope for the kingdom."

In like manner Arbaces, appointed prefect of Media by Sardanapalus, rebelled against that effeminate king, saying he could not obey one who preferred to be a woman rather than a man; wherefore he besieged him and drove him to burn himself, as I said above.

Now Judith was of the tribe of Simeon, as she herself asserts at chapter 9:2; she dwelt in Bethulia, which is in the tribe of Zebulun; she lived 105 years, as is clear from the end of the book; she was a matron noble both in birth and in beauty, widowed in status, flourishing in age, rich in wealth, outstanding in piety, whose extraordinary beauty rivalled her equal virtue, and whom therefore God chose for this heroic deed and the liberation of Israel. Bellarmine judges that when she slew Holofernes she was [a certain number] years of age; Serarius says 25, Torniel 53, Gretser 40, Salianus 33.

# GEM Hear St. Jerome, Epistle 10 to Furia: "We read of the widow Judith, worn out by fasts and defiled by mournful garb, who mourned not for her dead husband but sought in bodily squalor the coming of her Spouse. Behold her arm armed with a sword, her right hand dripping with blood; I recognize the head of Holofernes brought back from the midst of enemies. A woman conquers men, and chastity cuts off lust; and suddenly changing her garb, she returns to her victorious squalor, purer than all the adornments of the world."
The Fathers bestow extraordinary praises upon Judith. Clement of Alexandria calls her "consummate" or "perfect among women," in Book Four of the Stromata.

Judith is called magnificent and most noble of all women. Ambrose, book 3 *de Officiis*, c. 13, calls her "a wonderful woman." St. Fulgentius, *Epistle* 2, c. 14, frequently calls her a holy widow, illustrious by birth, rich in resources, young in age, wonderful in beauty, a despiser of riches, rejecting delights, trampling the incentives of the flesh, and clothed with virtue from on high. # GEM St. Ambrose, *de Viduis*: "Judith, strengthened by long mourning and daily fasts, is holy." St. Augustine, *Serm. de Temp.* 228: "Most holy Judith, at whose prayers heaven opened, who by the art of prayer forged victorious arms." And below: "Behold, prayer preserved the chastity of this glorious woman." # GEM St. Chrysostom, *hom.* 9 *ex variis*: "Most holy Judith, who while she concealed her fast with a cheerful countenance, won triumph and victory over the enemy." # GEM Tertullian, *de Monogamia*, c. 17: "Neither Judith, daughter of Merari, nor so many other holy women..."

Arphaxad king of the Medes had subjugated many nations under his rule, and he built a most powerful city which he called Ecbatana. The word "itaque" (therefore/and so) is partly a marker of the beginning of a history or book; for it denotes the Hebrew "vaw," that is, "and so" or "therefore," as I noted at Ezekiel 1:1. Thus Cicero sometimes begins his Letters in this way: "Tu igitur, tu vero, ego vero." It also signifies that this history was taken and excerpted from the ancient diaries or chronicles of the Hebrews, in which this history was appended to the histories of earlier times and connected to them by the word "itaque." So Serarius.

Arphaxad here, or Areapad, is the same as Arbaces. Hence Serarius and others understand Arphaxad to mean Arbaces, who, after killing Sardanapalus, made himself king of Media, concerning whom see more at v. 5. Others, with Bellarminus and Salianus, hold that Arphaxad is Diocles or Dejoces, who ruled the Medes in the time of Manasseh king of Judah. For Dejoces was succeeded by Phraortes, says Herodotus, Phraortes by Cyaxares, Cyaxares by Astyages the grandfather of Cyrus, although Ctesias in Diodorus assigns other and more kings of the Medes.

Now Dejoces built Ecbatana, according to Herodotus, Eusebius, and others, although Genebrardus takes Arphaxad here to mean Cyaxares, and Salianus takes him to mean Phraortes. Thirdly, others who think this history occurred under Xerxes believe Arbaces was the prefect of Media, who from Nineveh, or from Darius the son of Hystaspes, obtained the name and title of royal dignity; for all kings of Media took their name from the first king Arbaces and were called Arbaces or Arbacidae, just as Roman Emperors from Julius Caesar were called Caesars; the Egyptians from the first Ptolemy were called Ptolemies; the Greeks from the first king Seleucus were called Seleucidae; the Parthians from the first Arsaces were called Arsacidae.

Moreover the name Arphaxad in Hebrew means "one who weakens," or rather "one who brings depopulation," as Pagninus says, and aptly so. For he weakened, exhausted, and as it were depopulated his own people by his proud buildings and chariots, and therefore was despoiled and subjugated by Nebuchadnezzar.

HE BUILT A MOST POWERFUL CITY WHICH HE CALLED ECBATANA. The name Ecbatanis or Ecbatana is used sometimes as if indeclinable. That this city already existed before is established. Hence Diodorus, book 2, asserts it existed before Semiramis, saying that after Babylon was built she came here, erected magnificent palaces, and, since the city suffered from a scarcity of water, she diverted an abundance of water to it by cutting through Mount Orontes, which is twelve stades away.

Yet Arphaxad is said here to have built it, because he enlarged and adorned it with new palaces, towers, and walls. Hence the Greek has: "He built walls around Ecbatana of hewn stones." Thus Nebuchadnezzar says he founded Babylon, that is, adorned and enlarged it, Dan. 4:27. For it was Nimrod with his companions who first founded Babel and Babylon, Gen. 10. Thus Romulus is said to have founded Rome, although Virgil says in Aeneid 8: "Then king Evandrus, founder of the Roman citadel."

OF SQUARED AND CUT STONES. The Greek adds: three cubits broad and six cubits long. HE MADE ITS WALLS SEVENTY CUBITS IN BREADTH. The breadth of these walls was immense, such that many chariots could proceed and run along them simultaneously. AND IN HEIGHT THIRTY CUBITS. The Greek has fifty.

NOW AS TO THE SQUARE OF THE TOWERS, EACH SIDE EXTENDED OVER A SPACE OF TWENTY FEET. That is, each side of the towers with the side opposite and facing it, meaning each of the four individual sides of the towers was twenty feet. For it was a geometric square, all of whose sides are equal. For "twenty feet," the Greek has "sixty cubits."

AND HE GLORIED AS IF MIGHTY. This was the cause of the war, namely pride and the lust for dominion, such as existed among the ancient monarchs of the Assyrians, Babylonians, Persians, Greeks, and Romans; hence the civil war between Pompey and Julius Caesar broke out, because Pompey would not have Caesar as his equal, and Caesar would not have Pompey as his superior. Similar is the boast of that Thraso in Seneca, vaunting thus: "And as one greater than the stars, reaching the high heaven with proud head above all others; I dismiss the gods, I have touched the summit of my wishes. O me, most exalted of the heavenly ones, and king of kings! I have surpassed my own vows." # GEM

IN THE TWELFTH YEAR THEREFORE (this war began, but was finished in the seventeenth year, if we believe the Greek text, which says the war lasted six years) OF HIS REIGN, NEBUCHADNEZZAR. You will ask who was this Nebuchadnezzar? First, Zonaras and Genebrardus hold that he was that great Nebuchadnezzar who overthrew Jerusalem with the Temple and led the Jews into Babylon. But this king reigned in Babylon, not in Nineveh as is stated here, and he conquered the Jews, whereas here he is conquered by them through Judith.

Secondly, Bellarminus and Salianus hold him to be Merodach Baladan (whom Ptolemy in the Almagest calls Mardocempad), who indeed reigned in Babylon; but having defeated Esarhaddon king of Nineveh, he fixed the seat of his kingdom in Nineveh itself, as the Turk did at Constantinople after capturing it. For the name Nebuchadnezzar was a common name among the kings of the Chaldeans. Hence Nabonassar in the Alfonsine Tables, and by Albategnius, is always called Nebuchadnezzar. See Serarius here, question 4. For both names are the same or nearly the same; hence some think this Nebuchadnezzar was the father of that Nebuchadnezzar who was the father of Nabu—

Serarius adds, with whom Genebrardus, Pererius and others agree, that Asarhaddon, or Sarphedon as the Septuagint has it, is to be identified with Sardanapalus; wherefore this Nabuchodonosor was the son of Sardanapalus, who, wishing to avenge his father's death, made war upon Arbaces the governor of Media, who had driven his father to death and burning, and deprived him of both kingdom and life. Hence Arbaces here is called Arphaxad, but in Diodorus and Ctesias he is called Diocles.

Diodorus supports this (bk. 3, ch. 1), saying that Sardanapalus, when he saw the destruction of Nineveh threatened by Arbaces, sent his sons out of Nineveh to Paphlagonia to its governor Cottus, so that they might escape the hands of Arbaces. Hear Diodorus: "The king (Sardanapalus) being besieged in the city (Nineveh), many nations, out of desire for liberty, defected to Arbaces. The king, seeing that his kingdom was in great danger, sent three sons and two daughters with a great quantity of gold and silver to Cottus the governor of Paphlagonia." Among these three sons was this Nabuchodonosor, who, aided by the governor of Paphlagonia with an armed force, recovered Nineveh and his father's kingdom, defeated Arbaces and slew him.

Certainly if we say that the history of Judith took place before the Babylonian captivity under Manasses, this opinion seems very plausible: for then Asarhaddon reigned, and if he was Sardanapalus, then likewise Arbaces reigned in Media at that time; for after defeating and slaying Sardanapalus, he made himself king of Media.

But if we say that the history of Judith (as I said in the preface) took place after the Babylonian captivity, it must consequently be asserted that this Nabuchodonosor was either Cambyses the son of Cyrus, as Saint Augustine, Eusebius, Beda and others think — whence the Chronicon Alexandrinum says: "They say that Cambyses was called the second Nabuchodonosor among the Hebrews, because they were afflicted by both" — or Darius Hystaspis, as Sanchez maintains.

For Xerxes swelled so greatly with his forces and power that he believed he could command not only the land but also the waters and the sea. Hear Herodotus, book 7: "From Abydos to the further shore is a crossing of one stade, which when joined by bridges a great storm broke apart and dissolved. When Xerxes heard this, he was indignant and ordered three hundred lashes to be inflicted on the Hellespont and a pair of fetters to be cast into its sea. I have heard moreover that he also sent those who would brand the Hellespont with hot irons, speaking barbarous words: 'Master inflicts this punishment upon you, because you harmed him.'" Valerius Maximus likewise reports this (bk. 3, ch. 2) and adds that he further threatened heaven with darkness because it had been hostile and injurious to him. What could be more foolish, more insolent and more arrogant?

But because Xerxes reigned in Persia, not in Nineveh, and was friendly and well-disposed toward the Jews, as Josephus attests, so much so that his son Artaxerxes sent Ezra and Nehemiah back to Judaea to rebuild Jerusalem — which he would surely not have done if his father Xerxes had been so shamefully slain in his commander Holofernes by the Jews — it truly seems more likely that this Nabuchodonosor was one of the princes and governors of Xerxes, to whom on account of his merits, strength and power, Xerxes, or rather the father of Xerxes, Darius Hystaspis, granted the kingdom of Nineveh and Assyria, though under tribute; who, seeing Xerxes defeated by the Greeks, refused this tribute, made himself an absolute king, and gradually growing in arms, forces and peoples, invaded and overcame Arphaxad king of Media.

By origin and race he appears to have been a Babylonian (for his name Nabuchodonosor, which is Chaldaean, indicates this) and perhaps he was a grandson, or rather great-grandson, or a kinsman of Nabuchodonosor the Great, and for this reason usurped his name.

Moreover, Nabuchodonosor in Hebrew means "one who constrains a generation with distress," as Pagninus says in his Hebrew Nomenclator, and aptly so. For he constrained the Jews and other nations and reduced them to extremity, as is clear from what follows.

NABUCHODONOSOR KING OF THE ASSYRIANS, WHO REIGNED IN NINEVEH.] This is added to distinguish him from Nabuchodonosor the Great, who reigned in Babylon, and who therefore transferred the Monarchy from Nineveh and the Assyrians to Babylon and the Chaldaeans: although Nineveh was near to Babylon, and the Assyrians to the Chaldaeans, and therefore from the Assyrians arose the monarchy of the Chaldaeans; wherefore they are reckoned as one and the same. Hence also the name Nabuchodonosor is partly Chaldaean and partly Assyrian. For Nabo was the name of an idol of the Chaldaeans; Sar was the name of the Assyrian kings, as is evident in Salmanasar, Tiglathpileser, Asarhaddon, which are compounded from Sar.

HE WARRED AGAINST ARPHAXAD AND OVERCAME HIM,] that is, he captured him and pierced him with javelins. For the Greek reads thus: "Nabuchodonosor was strengthened in his war, and overthrew all the power of Arphaxad and all his cavalry and all his chariots; and he became master of his cities, and came even to Ecbatana, and took possession of its towers, and plundered its streets, and turned its splendour into its reproach. And he took Arphaxad in the mountains of Ragau, and pierced him through with his javelins, and utterly destroyed him, and exterminated him to that day."

This slaughter was merited by the proud boasting of Arphaxad in his forces and chariots: for, as Seneca says in Hercules: "The avenging God follows the proud from behind." The same in Thyestes: "You to whom the ruler of sea and land has given great power over death and life, lay aside your proud and haughty looks"; and shortly after: "He whom the fleeing day saw arrogant, the fleeing day saw prostrate: let no one trust too much in prosperity." # GEM

Wherefore Artabanus the king gave this prudent warning to Xerxes (under whom these things occurred, indeed whom Sanchez thinks was Nabuchodonosor): "You see how God strikes the great animals with lightning and does not permit them to think proud thoughts: but those which are small he does not touch at all. You see how he always hurls such bolts against the greatest buildings and trees? For God loves to cut down or lop off all that stands out above the rest." And the reason is given: "For he permits no one to think great things of himself, save himself alone."

JURAVIT PER THRONUM ET REGNUM SUUM] He thought that a great injury and ignominy had been inflicted upon him by the nations, and therefore, to recover and defend his honor wounded by them, he was obliged to avenge and punish this outrage by war and destruction: just as today many think that to defend their honor they are obliged to avenge and kill those who have injured it by some insult. But this is the paralogism of the world, and the lying spirit of the devil. To defend in this way...

Cretus (the Greek text) narrates thus: He himself returned with his forces to Nineveh, and the entire army that had gathered to him, a very great multitude of fighting men, and he was there at leisure and given to feasting, both he himself and his army, for one hundred and twenty days.

TUNC EXALTATUM EST REGNUM NABUCHODONOSOR, ET COR EJUS ELEVATUM EST] Considering himself stronger than all others, he was ambitious for monarchy, and resolved to subjugate all nations to himself. Truly Seneca in Troades: "Neither rage nor a fierce enemy nor victory can be restrained by reins, nor the prosperous sword, whose guilty pleasure is mad lust."

And he sent to all nations ambassadors demanding that all peoples submit to the conqueror Nabuchodonosor. These things are narrated differently in the Greek, namely that Nabuchodonosor before the victory over Arphaxad sent messengers to all neighboring nations demanding aid against Arphaxad; which being refused by the nations, he swore he would destroy them; then he fought with Arphaxad in the 17th year of his reign, defeated him, transfixed him with javelins, overthrew Ecbatana, then returned in triumph to Nineveh, where he gave himself over to leisure and luxury for 120 days.

But the Vulgate Latin edition must be authoritative for us, and the Greek must either be reconciled with it if possible, or if not possible, rejected. In this place it can be reconciled. For Nabuchodonosor could have sent messengers twice: first before the battle, as the Greek has it; second after the battle, as the Vulgate has it.

ET AD GENTES, QU/E SUNT IN CARMELO ET CEDAR, ET INHABITANTES GALIL/EAM] The Greek enumerates more nations. Hence it is clear that Nabuchodonosor was not Xerxes. For to Xerxes as monarch most nations obeyed, and especially the Galileans: for from Ezra and Nehemiah it is clear that Darius the father of Xerxes and Artaxerxes the son of Xerxes sent Nehemiah and other leaders into Judea, who governed the Jews in their name.

QUI OMNES UNO ANIMO CONTRADIXERUNT, ET REMISERUNT EOS VACUOS, ET SINE HONORE (nay with ignominy, as the Greek has it) ABJECERUNT] This was deserved all the more by the arrogance of Nabuchodonosor, now insolent from victory, so that just as he had laid low the arrogance of Arphaxad, so too his own insolence would be laid low by Judith, and he would be mocked by the nations.

JURAVIT PER THRONUM ET REGNUM SUUM, quod defenderet (that is, would avenge) se] # GEM For he thought a great injury and ignominy had been inflicted upon him by the nations, and therefore to recover and defend his honor wounded by them he was obliged to avenge this outrage by war and destruction: just as today many think that to defend their honor they are obliged to avenge and kill those who have injured them by some insult. But this is the paralogism of the world, and the lying spirit of the devil.
[SYNOPSIS OF CHAPTER 9] Nebuchadnezzar, with the approval of his war council, sends Holofernes to subjugate the western nations by force of arms.

Anno tertio decimo [In the thirteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar]. The Greek text has year 18. If you wish to reconcile these with one another, say that the thirteenth year of Nebuchadnezzar was the forty-eighth from the beginning of the reign of Arphaxad, his rival and enemy.

Habuit cum eis mysterium (that is, the secret or mystery) consilii sui [He held with them the secret of his counsel]. For the counsels of kings, especially concerning the subjugation of other kings and peoples, are secret and hidden.

Holofernem principem militiae suae [Holofernes the chief of his army]. You ask: Who was Holofernes, what was his character, and how great was he? I answer first: Cedrenus says he was a Persian, for the name appears to be Persian, just as Tissaphernes, Artaphernes, and Intaphernes are Persian names, although according to Serarius, book 1, p. 90, there are also found among the Assyrians, Armenians, and Cappadocians men named Holofernes. If he was a Persian, Nebuchadnezzar chose a Persian commander so that he might draw the Persians to himself — Persians who had already been made contemptible by Xerxes through the defeat received from the Greeks — and this he did. Hence in the last chapter, verse 12, it is said: The Persians feared his steadfastness, and the Medes his boldness, namely that of Judith; for after Arphaxad and the Medes had already been subdued by Nebuchadnezzar, many Medes and Persians neighboring the Medes had given their names to his military service.

Second, Holofernes was the commander of Nebuchadnezzar's army, and therefore second to him, as the Greek text states: just as Joseph was second to Pharaoh, Haman to Ahasuerus, Hephaestion to Alexander; hence Zonaras calls Holofernes the Archisatrap. Tertullian, however, in book 1 against Marcion...

Holofernes is called "king" because he had power of the most ample kind, and wealth almost royal. In a similar way Joseph, second to Pharaoh, is called by Joseph and Philo in Eusebius, lib. 9, Praeparat. Evang., ἀντίπρουχοι, that is, sceptre-bearer, which is to say king: for the sceptre is the mark of a king.
# GEM Third, Francis of Meyronnes asserts that Holofernes was the son of Ahasuerus and Esther; but all reject this as novel and unheard-of.

Fourth, Pagninus in his Hebrew Names says that Holofernes, according to Greek etymology, means as it were "total dowry": for ὅλος means "whole," φερνή means "dowry." There are also those who think it is a compound of Hebrew and Chaldean, and interpret it as "prefect of the army." For "pharnaspes" in Chaldean means prefect or leader. Thus Pagninus. Hence the names of illustrious Persian commanders are Pharnaspes, Pharnuches, Pharnabas.

Fifth, regarding his character, Holofernes in pride and arrogance, as well as in dissoluteness and luxury, was similar to Nebuchadnezzar, and therefore he was conquered and laid low by Judith, as will be evident from what follows. The Greek adds that Nebuchadnezzar said to Holofernes that he would follow with his entire forces: "I will go out," he says, "in my fury against them, and will cover the whole earth with the feet of my army," etc.

AND HE SAID TO HIM: GO FORTH AGAINST EVERY KINGDOM OF THE WEST. The Greek narrates this royal command more fully, and says: "These things says the great King, Lord of all the earth: Behold, you shall go forth from my presence, and you shall take with you men who trust in their own strength, infantry up to one hundred and twenty thousand, and a multitude of horses with their riders — a multitude, I say, of twelve myriads, or one hundred and twenty thousand — and you shall go out to meet, or fight, every land that lies to the west, because they have not obeyed the word of my mouth. And you shall declare to them that they should prepare earth and water, because I will go out in my fury against them, and will cover the whole face of the earth with the feet of my army, and I will give them up to plunder, and their slain shall fill the valleys and torrents. And even the river, overflowing with their dead, shall be filled; and I will at last lead their captivity, or drive it, to the ends of all the earth. But you, going forth, shall seize for me all their borders beforehand, and they will surrender themselves to you, and you shall reserve them for me until the day of their punishment. But against those who are rebellious and disobedient, your eye shall not spare them, but you shall give them up to slaughter and plunder throughout all your land; because as I live and as is the strength of my kingdom, what I have spoken, that also will I do with my hand. But you shall not transgress even a single word of your Lord's commands, but you shall fully execute as I have commanded you: and you shall not delay at all in doing these things."

Note in these words of the king the phrase "you shall declare to them that they prepare earth and water for me," by which is signified the ancient custom of the Persians, who, wishing to subjugate some nation and demanding that it surrender itself to the Persians, would require from it earth and water as a symbol of servitude. For these things are necessary for human life. Hence Aristotle, Rhetoric bk. 2, says: "To provide earth and water is to be a servant." Hence when Darius Hystaspis demanded earth and water from the Spartans as a sign of surrender, the Spartans threw his ambassadors into a well, telling them to draw earth and water from there and carry it to Darius.

And twelve thousand mounted archers. The Greek has 120,000, as we have already heard; where there seems to be an error in the number, and the cipher 0 ought to be deleted, so that 12,000 remain, as our text has. For who ever led so many thousands of horsemen into battle? Indeed Darius led against Alexander only 45,000 cavalry and 200,000 infantry, says Curtius, bk. 4—unless one were to say that the mounted archers here numbered 12,000, while the remaining horsemen armed with lances, sarissas, battle-axes, etc., numbered 108,000, so that both together would make 120,000. Perhaps in the Greek, with the hundred removed, one should read 20,000, of which 12,000 were archers, as the Greek also has, the rest being of another armament.

He came to the great mountains of Ange, which are on the left side of Cilicia. Ange therefore was the name then given to the mountains which were on the left of Cilicia, from which sprang Philtes the Angean, mentioned by Curtius bk. 3; in Herodotus bk. 7 a great mountain called Rangaeus is named, and so by others.

He broke through (stormed) moreover the most renowned city (the most famous, and most celebrated for wealth and strength) of Melothi (Melothi in Hebrew means fullness, namely of wealth and men: such indeed was Melothi) and plundered all the sons of Tharsis. These are those of whom it is said in 2 Macc. 4:30: It came to pass that the Tarsians and Mallotans raised a sedition, because they had been given as a gift to Antiochis, a concubine of the king. For Tharsis was a grandson of Japheth, Gen. 10, from whom the Mediterranean Sea, and Cilicia as its boundary, and thence any sea whatsoever, was called Tharsis, as I said on Isaiah 2:16.
Mambre. The Greek has Arbonai.
Even unto the borders of Japheth. This signifies Joppe, says Serarius, which is also called today Japha, and by the Turks Zapha: for, as Pliny says, bk. 5...

Concerning the demand for earth and water as a sign of submission: as Herodotus narrates, bk. 7. Hence Xerxes the son of Darius did not dare to send envoys to the Spartans to demand earth and water from them, as St. Jerome says, bk. 8, and Diodorus bk. 11. Hence Alexander the Great, in Curtius bk. 3, about to fight against Darius Codomannus and the Persians, in his speech to the soldiers reproaches this arrogance of Darius Hystaspis and Xerxes, that they should remember they were fighting against Persians who were accustomed to demand earth and water: so that to those who submitted to them they would leave neither the draught of springs nor food, the fruits of the earth. Again Herodotus bk. 4 narrates that Darius Hystaspis demanded earth and water from Idanthyrsus the leader of the Scythians. And when he sent a bird, a mouse, a frog, and five arrows, Darius's interpretation was that the Scythians were surrendering themselves to him, both land and water—because the mouse is born in the earth and lives on the same food as men; the frog is born in the waters; the bird moreover is likened to a horse; but by giving the arrows they were surrendering their own strength.
["And when it was late"] The banquet ended and all withdrew. Holofernes, heavy with wine, fell into a deep sleep on his bed.
Judith was left alone in the tent, as Abra her maidservant waited outside.
Judith stood before the bed praying silently, moving her lips, saying: "Strengthen me, O Lord God of Israel, in this hour."
She struck his neck twice with all her might and severed his head.
She rolled his body off the bed and took down the canopy from the pillars. Shortly after she went out and gave the head of Holofernes to her maidservant.
["And she put it in her bag"] That is, in the wallet or pouch in which she carried her food, as was said above.
["And they went out"] Both women went out together, as was their custom, to pray, so that no one suspected anything unusual.
["And they passed through the camp"] They passed through without hindrance, since Judith was known to the guards as one who regularly went out to pray.
["And having compassed the valley"] Greek: περιελθοῦσαι, that is, having gone around, they ascended the hill to Bethulia.
["And the men of the city"] The watchmen on the walls saw them and called out, marveling that they had returned safely.
["Open the gates"] Judith called out to the gatekeepers to open, saying that God was with them and had shown His power in Israel.
["And it came to pass"] When the men heard her voice, they called the elders of the city with haste.
["And they all ran together"] Both small and great, for none doubted that something wonderful had occurred.
["And she said to them with a loud voice"] Judith spoke clearly and boldly, attributing all to God and not to herself. # GEM
["Praise ye the Lord our God"] She first gives thanks to God before showing them the head, which is an example of piety and gratitude. The glory of the deed she refers entirely to God.
["And she brought forth the head"] Greek: ἐξεκύκλωσε, that is, she uncovered, unwrapped, displayed it from the bag to all the people assembled.

["And she said"] Judith then explained what had happened: that Holofernes had been slain not by his own blood, but that God had struck him down through the hand of a woman, to the confusion of the enemy. Greek adds here: He took away the flocks and herds, plundered the cities, devastated the fields, and put all their young men to the sword.
[He cut down their groves] along with the gods and idols which, in the manner of the nations, were worshipped in groves, concealing their obscenities, as is said in the book of Kings.

[That he might destroy all gods, that he alone might be called God.] See to what height the pride of this man rises, who, forgetting that he is earth and dust, makes war upon God and divinity, and wishes himself to be held and worshipped as God, indeed as the only God. And he did this also because he had conquered Arphaxad. Truly Darius says in Curtius, book 4: "Human frailty," he says, "is excessive forgetfulness in times of prosperity." Similar was the condition of Lucifer (Isaiah 14, v. 15), and of Pharaoh (Ezekiel 29, v. 3), and of the King of Tyre (Ezekiel 28), and therefore all of them deserved to be laid low and cast down from their kingdom and their life. # GEM Pride therefore is sheer madness, which makes a man like a Titan — not merely a fighter against the giants, but also a fighter against God (theomachos), so as to war against God.

Now when the children of Israel who dwelt in the land of Judah heard these things, they feared greatly before his face. The Greeks add more fully: "And they feared greatly before his face, and were troubled for Jerusalem and the temple of the Lord their God, because they had recently come up from captivity, and all the people had lately been gathered out of Judaea, and the vessels, and the altar, and the house of God (that is, the temple) had been sanctified after profanation." Many, as I said in the proem, together with Bellarmine, Salian, and others, refer these things to Manasseh king of Judah, who, having been taken captive with his people to Babylon, was released and returned to his kingdom, and in penitence purged the temple which he had profaned by idols, and restored it to the worship of the true God. But this was also a captivity and liberation of Manasseh the king, not of the whole people. Add that no mention is made of King Manasseh throughout this entire book, which surely ought to have been made, since it pertained to him as king rather than to Eliachim the high priest to fortify Judaea against Holofernes and to garrison the mountain passes before the enemy could enter. Wherefore others more correctly refer these things to the times of Xerxes, when the temple had already been rebuilt after being profaned and burned by the Chaldeans.

One may ask: How did the Jews, having recently returned from captivity, possess such great strength and courage as to dare to oppose themselves against such numerous and powerful armies? I answer that this was done from fear of captivity, whose bitterness and miseries they had lately experienced in Babylon, and from fear of losing the true religion. For Nebuchadnezzar threatened the destruction of God and the temple, because he wished to be adored alone as God. But to the Jews, who worshipped the true God, this seemed intolerable, so that they preferred to die rather than permit it. Moreover, the progeny of the Jews was innumerable, multiplying by generation like frogs and fishes, as was the case in the time of Moses, Exodus 1. For Josephus, book 11 of the Antiquities, chapter 4, numbers those who returned from the Babylonian captivity at four thousand thousand of adult males in the second year of Darius Hystaspes, and beyond that 620 thousand, and that from the tribes of Judah and Benjamin alone, to whom very many from the other ten tribes had joined themselves. Therefore they could easily gather a numerous army to oppose Holofernes, especially in the passes and precipitous places of the mountains, where one man is worth ten in resisting enemies.

Tremor and horror invaded their senses. The Greeks have more fully: "And they feared greatly before his face, and were troubled for Jerusalem and the temple of the Lord their God, because they had recently come up from captivity." # GEM The terror of the Jews was twofold: first, for themselves and their families; second, and more especially, for the temple of God and the holy things thereof. For they who truly love God fear more for God's honour than for their own safety, as Augustine teaches: "The man who loves God more than himself trembles more at the dishonour of God than at his own destruction."

They sent into all Samaria round about, even to Jericho, and they seized beforehand all the tops of the mountains. The Jews thus acted with great prudence, occupying the mountain passes before Holofernes could seize them, for in such narrow defiles a small force can hold back a great army, as one man in a narrow bridge can resist a multitude.

And they compassed the villages with walls and gathered corn in preparation for war. For the fields had recently been harvested, as the Greek adds. Prudence requires that in time of peace one prepare for war, according to the proverb: "If you wish for peace, prepare for war." The Jews therefore did wisely in gathering provisions while they could, lest a prolonged siege reduce them to famine.

And the priest Eliachim wrote to all who were over against Esdrelon, which is over against the face of the great plain near Dothaim, and to all by whom a passage might be made. Esdrelon, otherwise called the plain of Jezreel, was a great and wide valley in the heart of Judaea, through which the principal roads passed. Dothaim, or Dothan, was the place where Joseph was sold by his brothers, Genesis 37, and where Elisha the prophet was surrounded by the Syrian army, 2 Kings 6.

That they should keep the passages of the hills, by which there could be any way to Jerusalem, and should guard where the narrow passes were between the mountains. The passes here mentioned are principally those of Mount Carmel and the mountains of Samaria, through which Holofernes must march if he would attack Jerusalem. Eliachim wisely foresaw that if these passes were held, Holofernes could not easily bring his great army against the holy city.

And the children of Israel did as the priest of the Lord Eliachim had appointed them. They obeyed their high priest readily and unanimously, which is commendable. For in times of common danger, unity of counsel and obedience to lawful authority are most necessary for safety. This is the way to conquer: when the people obey their leaders, and their leaders obey God.

And all the people cried to the Lord with great earnestness, and they humbled their souls in fastings and prayers, both they and their wives. Prayer and fasting are the true weapons of the Church militant against her enemies. When human forces are insufficient, recourse must be had to God, who alone can save. # GEM As Bernard says: "The Church conquers by praying, not by fighting; she overcomes by humility, not by pride."

And the priests put on haircloth, and they caused the infants to prostrate themselves before the face of the temple of the Lord, and the altar of the Lord they covered with haircloth. Sackcloth was a garment of rough hair worn as a sign of penance and humiliation before God. That even the infants were prostrated before the temple signifies that the whole people, without exception of age, devoted itself to supplication. The covering of the altar with sackcloth signified that even the very worship of God mourned, as it were, and implored the divine mercy.

And they cried to the Lord the God of Israel with one accord, that their children might not be given as a prey, and their wives into destruction, and their cities into ruin, and their holy things into profanation, and that they might not be made a reproach to the Gentiles. This prayer is most fitting and ordered with great wisdom. The Jews do not pray merely for their own safety, but chiefly that God's holy things might not be profaned and His name dishonoured among the Gentiles. So ought Christians always to pray: first for God's glory, then for their own needs.

Then Eliachim the great priest of the Lord went about all Israel and spoke to them. It belongs to the office of the high priest and of all pastors to encourage the people in time of tribulation, to strengthen the faint-hearted, and to point them to God as their only refuge. Eliachim here fulfils admirably the duty of a true shepherd.

Saying: Know ye that the Lord will hear your prayers, if you continue with perseverance in fastings and prayers in the sight of the Lord. # GEM The condition laid down by Eliachim is most important: "if you continue with perseverance." A prayer that is brief and soon abandoned obtains little. God wishes to be urged and pressed by persevering prayer, not because He is unwilling to give, but because He wishes to increase our desire and our merit. As Gregory says: "God defers to increase our desire, he does not deny to diminish our hope."

Be mindful of Moses the servant of the Lord, who overcame Amalek that trusted in his own strength, and in his power, and in his army, and in his shields, and in his chariots, and in his horsemen, not by fighting with the sword, but by holy prayers. This example of Moses is most apt. When Moses held up his hands in prayer during the battle with Amalek, Israel prevailed; when his hands dropped, Amalek prevailed, Exodus 17. Thus Eliachim teaches that prayer is mightier than the sword, and that victory comes from God alone, not from human strength.

So shall it be with all the enemies of Israel, if you persevere in this work which you have begun. The promise is conditional upon perseverance. God does not abandon those who do not abandon prayer. The history of Israel bears perpetual witness to this truth: when the people prayed, they conquered; when they neglected prayer, they were conquered.

At this exhortation of his, the people continued in prayer before the Lord. The people responded well to the exhortation of their high priest, which shows both their piety and the efficacy of good pastoral teaching. A faithful people follows a faithful pastor; and where the shepherd leads to prayer, the flock follows to salvation.

So that even they that offered the holocausts to the Lord, offered them girded with haircloth, and with ashes upon their heads. This is a remarkable expression of penance: even in the very act of offering sacrifice to God, the priests wore sackcloth and ashes. This teaches that external worship must be accompanied by internal humility and contrition. God is not pleased with sacrifice without a contrite heart, as the Psalmist says: "A sacrifice to God is an afflicted spirit."

And with all their heart all prayed to God that he would visit his people Israel. This concluding verse summarizes the entire spiritual effort of the Israelites: unanimous, wholehearted prayer for God's merciful visitation of His people. The word "visit" here means a visitation of mercy and salvation, as when God visited His people in Egypt to deliver them. So the Jews pray that God would look down upon their misery and come to their aid.

The peoples of the surrounding nations, terrified by the destruction of their neighbors, send ambassadors to Holofernes offering complete submission, saying: "Behold, we are the servants of Nebuchadnezzar the great king; we lie prostrate before thee: do with us as is good in thy sight."

They offer themselves as slaves, their lands, their cities, their flocks and herds, and all their possessions, to be disposed of as Holofernes pleases, in order to avoid the fate of those who had resisted.
They declare that all their cities and inhabitants are ready to serve Nebuchadnezzar, their fields and flocks and all their wealth lying open to him without resistance.
The ambassadors come to Holofernes at Cilicia and announce that the peoples are ready to submit entirely, seeking only that their lives be spared.
Holofernes accepts their submission and stations garrisons throughout the coastal region, appointing chosen men from every tribe as auxiliaries.

He and all his army advance in great triumph through the plain, destroying the high places and sacred groves, for he had orders to destroy all the gods of the land, so that all nations should worship Nebuchadnezzar alone.
The peoples of those cities receive Holofernes with garlands and dances and tambourines, yet he destroys their sacred places nevertheless.

Lapide notes that Holofernes destroys all the temples and cuts down all the sacred groves, having been commanded to destroy all gods so that Nebuchadnezzar alone should be called god by every nation and tongue. # GEM
He comes toward Esdraelon, near Dothan, which faces the great ridge of Judea, and camps between Geba and Scythopolis, and remains there a full month to provision his army.

Scarcity more often than battle destroys an army, and famine is fiercer than the sword. In every campaign there is one supreme counsel: that your own provisions suffice, while want breaks the enemy. Before war is begun, therefore, a careful reckoning must be made of supplies and expenditure, so that fodder, grain, and the other kinds of provisions which custom demands from the provincials may be requisitioned in good time, and in places convenient for operations and well fortified a store always larger than what is needed may be accumulated. And cap. 26: He who does not prepare grain and the necessary supplies is conquered without a sword.
Sacerdos etiam Eliachim — The Greek reads Joachim.

The name Eliachim [Joakim] signifies two things, namely 'stator of God' or 'stationary,' that is, one who made the battle lines of the Jews stand firm against Holofernes. For God is called both EL and NIA, which is an abbreviation of Jehova. Hence it is clear that this occurred in the time of Xerxes. For then Joakim was High Priest, as I said in the preface, who administered the commonwealth in the absence of a king: for as Josephus says in book 2 against Apion, the constitution of the Jewish commonwealth was generally entrusted to the priests, so that all the greatest matters were administered [by them].

Note here the use, manner, and power of public prayer and penance in public calamity, to be imitated by Christians, so that they may similarly avert their calamities. Namely: first, all the people cried out with great urgency; second, they humbled their souls in fastings and prayers; third, they clothed themselves in sackcloth; fourth, they prostrated the infants before the Lord and the temple; fifth, they covered the altar with sackcloth, so that by this public mourning of religion, justice, temple, and altar they might provoke God to pity and come to their aid — as if, by a kind of mob action and violence, as Tertullian says in the Apology, they might compel Him. The Ninevites did the same in Jonah 2, and the Maccabees in 1 Maccabees 3 and chapter 4, and therefore all merited to be heard and delivered by God.

The Greek has more fully: The people were fasting (and praying while fasting) for many days throughout all Judaea and Jerusalem before the face of the Holy Things, that is, the Holy of Holies — namely the temple. For those who were in Jerusalem to pray would go to the temple; but those who were outside would turn their faces toward the temple.

THEN ELIACHIM THE GREAT PRIEST OF THE LORD WENT ROUND ALL ISRAEL — so that he might exhort all to steadfastness, confidence, and perseverance in prayers and fasts, promising them victory if they did so. The precepts of Christ, says St Ambrose (Sermon 86), are arms for Christians, and divine fear drives from us the terror of the enemy. Now these are our arms with which the Saviour has equipped us: prayer, mercy, and fasting. For fasting protects better than a wall; mercy delivers more readily than plunder; prayer wounds from farther away than an arrow. For an arrow strikes only a nearby visible adversary, but prayer wounds an enemy placed far away. # GEM

The Greek has more fully here: Joachim the high priest, and all the priests who stood before the Lord, and the rest who ministered to the Lord, girded their loins with sackcloth and offered the continual burnt offering and vows, and the voluntary offerings of the people; and dust was upon their tiaras or mitres, and they cried out to the Lord with all their strength for good — that is, that He might visit to do good and to help all the house of Israel. Let bishops and princes imitate this pontiff: let them go round, visit, animate, instruct, and correct the peoples committed to their charge.

REMEMBER MOSES THE SERVANT OF THE LORD, WHO DEFEATED AMALEK, etc. — See the history in Exodus 17, where I have explained it. Note: Eliachim, no less than Moses, here prescribes two things for war: first, that they invoke God and seek and expect victory from Him; second, that they fortify their cities, occupy the passes, and gather all human resources for resistance. For God does not help the idle, but those who labour and fight. The pagan Cato saw this, as quoted by Sallust in the Catilinarian War, saying: Not by vows, nor by womanish supplications are the aids of the gods obtained; by watching, by acting, by taking good counsel, all things turn out prosperously. That is to say: The Thunderer on high helps not him who thunders, but him who strives.

And it was announced to Holofernes, prince of the army of the Assyrians, that the children of Israel had prepared themselves to resist, and had shut up the passes of the mountains. Holofernes is informed that the Israelites are preparing resistance and have blocked the mountain roads.

And he burned with exceeding great fury and wrath, and called all the princes of Moab and the leaders of Ammon. His rage is kindled at the audacity of this people in daring to resist the unconquerable army of Assyria.

And he said to them: Tell me who is this people that dwelleth in the mountains, and what are their cities, and of what sort and how great is their power, and what is their multitude, and what is their king over their army. Holofernes asks six questions concerning the Israelites, showing how entirely unknown to him this people was.

And why they above all that dwell in the east, have despised us, and have not come out to meet us, that they might receive us with peace? He is astonished that this people alone among all eastern nations dared to contemn him, for all others had submitted without resistance.

Then Achior, captain of all the children of Ammon, answering, said: If thou wilt vouchsafe, my lord, to hear, I will tell the truth in thy sight concerning this people that dwelleth in these mountains, and there shall not a false word come out of my mouth. Achior, a prudent and upright man, promises to speak the whole truth faithfully, whatever the consequences for himself.
This people is of the race of the Chaldeans. He begins the history of the Israelites from their very origin, going back to Abraham their father, who was a Chaldean by birth and nation.

They dwelt first in Mesopotamia, because they would not follow the gods of the Chaldeans. Abraham and his family first dwelt in Mesopotamia, that is, in Haran, because they refused to worship the false gods of the Chaldeans, and were therefore driven out or chose to depart.

And forsaking the ceremonies of their fathers, which consisted in the worship of many gods, That is, they abandoned the polytheism of the Chaldeans and the whole system of idolatrous rites which their ancestors had practised.

They worshipped one God of heaven, who commanded them to depart thence, and to dwell in Charan. And when a famine had spread over all the earth, they went down into Egypt, and there for four hundred years were so multiplied that their army could not be numbered. Here Achior briefly recounts the calling of Abraham, the migration to Canaan, the descent into Egypt on account of famine, and the miraculous multiplication of the people there, in a summary of the entire patriarchal and Egyptian history. # GEM

And when the king of Egypt oppressed them, and made them work in clay and brick in building his cities, they cried to their Lord, and he struck all the land of Egypt with divers plagues. The oppression under Pharaoh and the ten plagues of Egypt are here briefly recalled, showing that God fights for His people when they cry to Him in their affliction.

And when the Egyptians had cast them out from them, and the plague had ceased from them, and they again desired to take them, and bring them back to their service, After the Israelites departed, Pharaoh repented of letting them go and pursued them with his whole army to the Red Sea.

The God of heaven opened the sea to them as they fled, so that the waters were made solid on either side, and they walked through the bottom of the sea on dry foot. The miraculous division of the Red Sea is here described: the waters stood as walls on both sides, and the Israelites passed through on dry ground, one of the greatest miracles in all Scripture. # GEM

In which place, when an innumerable army of Egyptians pursued after them, they were so covered with the waters that there was not one left to tell what had happened to posterity. The entire army of Pharaoh was swallowed up in the sea, not one survivor remaining, so that there was none to carry news of the catastrophe back to Egypt.

And after they came out of the Red Sea, they occupied the deserts of mount Sina, in which never man could dwell, nor son of man rested. After crossing the sea they entered the wilderness of Sinai, a barren and terrible desert, uninhabitable by nature.

There the bitter fountains were made sweet for them to drink, and for forty years they received food from heaven. God sweetened the bitter waters of Marah for them, and fed them with manna from heaven for forty years in the desert: singular miracles of divine providence. # GEM

Wherever they went in, without bow and arrow, and without shield and sword, their God fought for them and conquered. Throughout all their campaigns of conquest they prevailed not by human arms or military strength, but by the power of God alone fighting on their behalf.

And there was no one that insulted this people, except when they departed from the worship of the Lord their God. This is the key observation of Achior's whole speech: the Israelites were invincible only so long as they remained faithful to God; when they sinned they were overcome.

But as often as besides their own God, they worshipped any other, they were given to spoil, and to the sword, and to reproach. Every time they fell into idolatry they were defeated, plundered, and put to shame by their enemies — a clear law of divine justice running through all their history. # GEM

But as often as they were penitent for having revolted from the worship of their God, the God of heaven gave them power to resist. Equally, every time they repented and returned to God, He restored their strength and they triumphed over their enemies: a pattern repeated many times in the book of Judges and the history of the kings.

So they overthrew the king of the Canaanites, and the Jebusites, and the Pherezites, and the Hethites, and the Hevites, and the Amorrhites, and all the mighty men in Hesebon, and they possessed their lands and their cities. Achior enumerates the nations conquered by Israel under Joshua and the judges, all of which fell before this people when God was with them.

And as long as they sinned not in the sight of their God, it was well with them: for their God hateth iniquity. The theological principle underlying all Israelite history is stated plainly: God hates sin and punishes it with defeat, but blesses fidelity with victory and prosperity. # GEM

For even some years before, when they had revolted from the way which God had given them to walk in, they were destroyed in battles by many nations, and very many of them were led away captive into a strange land. This refers to the Babylonian captivity and other chastisements, by which God punished the apostasy of His people by delivering them into the hands of the Assyrians, Babylonians, and other nations.

But lately returning to the Lord their God, from the various places wherein they were scattered, they are come together and are gone up into all these mountains, and possess Jerusalem again, where their holy things are. Achior notes the recent return from the Babylonian captivity under Cyrus and the restoration of Jerusalem, showing that God has again reconciled Himself to His people.

Now therefore, my lord, search if there be any iniquity of this people in the sight of their God: if there be, let us go up to them, because God will deliver them to thee, and they shall be subdued under the yoke of thy power. Achior draws the practical conclusion: if the Jews have sinned against God, attack them and you will conquer; God Himself will hand them over to you.

But if this people have not offended their God, we shall not be able to resist them: because their God will defend them. The converse conclusion: if they are innocent before God, no human power can overcome them, for God Himself will be their shield and defender — a conclusion which the proud Holofernes will not tolerate.

[FILII ISRAEL PRAEPARAENT SE AD RESISTENDUM.] The Greek adds: "And they had placed in the fields scandula," that is, obstacles, such as hidden ditches, thorny barriers, and sharp stakes, into which the Assyrians passing carelessly would fall or stumble.

[ET DIXIT EIS: DICITE MIHI QUIS SIT POPULUS ISTE?] From this again it is gathered that these events occurred not under Manasseh but under Xerxes. For under Manasseh, and from Manasseh and the chief captives of the Jews, the Babylonians and Assyrians had sufficiently learned who and what the people of the Jews were. Hence those who wish these events to have occurred under Manasseh say that this questioning by Holofernes was not from ignorance, but from wonder and indignation, and as it were contemning the Jews as base.
[ET QUARE PRE OMNIBUS QUI HABITANT IN ORIENTE.] The Greek has: "in the West."

[TUNC ACHIOR DUX OMNIUM FILIORUM AMMON.] Serarius includes under the sons of Ammon also the Idumeans. For since they were brothers of the Jews (for Jacob, parent of the Jews, was brother to Esau, or Edom, parent of the Idumeans), when they wished to attack them, they called themselves Ammonites, not Idumeans, lest they be openly ill-spoken of for attacking their brothers. Hence in 2 Chron. 20:1 it is said: "There gathered together Moab and the sons of Ammon, and with them some of the Ammonites against Jehoshaphat." Where you see the Ammonites distinguished from the sons of Ammon. Where that book of Traditions says: "He means to understand by Ammonites the Idumeans, who out of reverence for the paternal name were unwilling to take up arms against Israel in their customary garb, but disguised themselves in the dress of the Ammonites." # GEM
[POPULUS ISTE EX PROGENIE CHALDAEORUM EST,] because God called Abraham, the father of the Hebrews, out of the land of the Chaldeans.

They descended into Egypt, and there were multiplied for about 400 years. Count these 400 years from Abraham's departure from Chaldea and Haran: for otherwise the Hebrews dwelt in Egypt only 215 years, as I showed at Exodus 12:40.
Covered by the waters — Pharaoh with all his men. Achior recounts the miracles by which God led the Hebrews from Egypt into Canaan, all of which I have explained in Exodus.

And there was none that insulted this people, except when they departed from the worship of the Lord their God. Learn here to serve God: for thus God will make you unconquerable and superior to all enemies. But if not, He will permit you to be subjected and trampled by any foe whatsoever. Let cities and kingdoms, and their kings and princes, learn the same. Hear the weighty maxims of two Fathers on this matter: one is St. Jerome, Epistle 13: "By our sins the barbarians are made strong; by our vices the Roman army is put to flight." The other is St. Ambrose, Sermon 85: "Destruction is brought upon a city only on account of the sins of its citizens. Cease to sin, and the city will not perish." # GEM Therefore peoples who sin must fear God's vengeance and destruction; but those who do not sin may hope for security and happiness.

For even before these years, when they had departed from the way (of religion and the law) which God had given them, etc., they were led away captive. By Nebuchadnezzar into Babylon — which was the most celebrated, universal, and properly Jewish captivity, from which they returned under Cyrus; such was not that which occurred under Manasseh, for that was not universal. The other captivity of the ten tribes under Hezekiah, carried out by Shalmaneser, was likewise not universal, because it was not of all, namely the twelve tribes, but only ten. Moreover that captivity was perpetual, because the Israelites never returned from it.

The Greek adds: "And the temple of their God was made into a pavement" — Vatablus says, leveled with the ground, because it was burned by the Chaldeans. Hence it is clear that this occurred after the destruction of Jerusalem. But Bellarmine, Serarius, and others, who hold that these things happened before the destruction, reply that these words were inserted into the Greek text by someone, since they are absent from the Latin, and therefore should be given little weight. Others reply that Manasseh by his idolatry despised, cast down, and by force profaned the temple, and indeed in many places demolished it and as it were leveled it to the ground.
We cannot resist them, because their God will defend them. Now if God is for us,
